Build a Blazor page for a team messaging app. This is a complex multi-panel
layout with a sidebar, main area, and collapsible thread panel. Here are the service contracts
already registered in DI:

```csharp
public record Channel(int Id, string Name, string Description, int UnreadCount);
public record Message(int Id, int ChannelId, string Author, string Content, DateTime SentAt, int? ReplyToId, List<Reaction> Reactions);
public record Reaction(string Emoji, List<string> Users);
public record ThreadSummary(int RootMessageId, int ReplyCount, DateTime LastReplyAt);

public interface IChatService
{
    Task<List<Channel>> GetChannelsAsync();
    Task<List<Message>> GetMessagesAsync(int channelId, int skip, int take);
    Task<Message> SendMessageAsync(int channelId, string content, int? replyToId);

    Task AddReactionAsync(int messageId, string emoji);
    Task MarkChannelReadAsync(int channelId);
}
```

The page layout should have:

**Left sidebar (channel list, about 20% width):**

- The active channel is highlighted
- Clicking a channel loads its messages in the main area and marks it as read

**Main message area (about 55% width, or 80% if no thread is open):**
- A header showing the current channel name and description
- A scrollable message list showing messages for the selected channel
- Each message displays: author name, timestamp (relative — "2 min ago"), message content, and a row of reaction emoji badges (each showing emoji + count)

- Messages that are thread starters show a "N replies" link below them
- A message input area at the bottom: a textarea and a "Send" button
- Load older messages on scroll-to-top (pagination via skip/take)

**Right panel (thread view, about 25% width, shown when a thread is open):**
- Header: "Thread" with the original message quoted and a close button
- A scrollable list of reply messages in the thread
- A reply input area at the bottom (textarea + "Reply" button)
- Closing the thread panel widens the main message area back

  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes

```
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |

| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

```
  │

  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard

  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
```

4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)

name: plan-ui-change
description: >
  Plan complex Blazor UI features by decomposing them into focused components.
  USE FOR: building a complex Blazor page with multiple sections, planning
  component decomposition, designing a multi-section dashboard or layout,
  breaking down a large UI feature into composable components, pages with
  sidebars and content panels, any page with 3+ distinct visual sections
  or multiple interacting sub-features, identifying parent-child relationships
  and data flow.
  DO NOT USE FOR: creating new Blazor projects or apps from scratch
  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown
## Component Plan: [Feature Name]

### Component Tree
[ASCII tree showing parent-child relationships]

### Component Table
| Component | Action | Render Mode | Purpose | Est. Lines |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Data Flow
[State owner] → [Parameters down] → [EventCallbacks up]

### Implementation Order
1. [First file to create — why]
2. [Second file — why]
...
```

After outputting the plan, **immediately begin implementing** the components in the order listed. Do not wait for approval or ask "shall I proceed?" — the plan is a guide for you to follow, not a proposal for the user to approve.

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

| Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | Correct Approach |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| One page component with 500+ lines | Impossible to test, reuse, or maintain | Decompose into focused components |
| Passing 10+ parameters through intermediate components | Parameter drilling obscures intent | Use cascading values or a scoped state service |
| Child component fetching its own data from an API | Multiple components making redundant calls | Parent owns data, passes via parameters |
| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |
| Generic components for one-off usage | Over-engineering adds complexity | Only extract generics when reuse is proven |

## Guidelines

- **Plan briefly, then implement.** Write a concise component table and data flow map, then immediately create the `.razor` files — never stop at just the plan.
- **Prefer many small components over one large one.** A component with a single clear purpose is easier to understand, test, and reuse.
- **State ownership is the first decision.** Before writing fetch logic, decide which component owns the data.
- **Build bottom-up.** Create leaf components first so parent components can reference them immediately.
- **Name components after what they render**, not what they do internally: `ProductCard` not `ProductRenderer`, `OrderFilters` not `FilterHandler`.

## Component Plan: Team Messaging

### Component Tree
```
ChatPage                    (page — owns all state, orchestrates layout)
├── ChannelSidebar          (channel list, active selection)
│   └── ChannelItem         (single channel row with name, desc, badge)
├── MessageArea             (main message panel)
│   ├── MessageList         (scrollable message list)
│   │   └── MessageItem     (single message with reactions, actions)
│   └── MessageInput        (textarea + send button)


└── ThreadPanel             (collapsible thread view)
    └── MessageInput        (reused — textarea + reply button)
```

### Data Flow
ChatPage manages the core state: channels, selected channel, messages, active thread, and thread messages. ChannelSidebar receives the channel list and selection state, emitting events when a channel is selected. MessageArea handles the main message display and input, emitting events for thread opens, message sends, reactions, and pagination. ThreadPanel displays the thread conversation and reuses the message input component for replies.

### Implementation Order
Starting with the leaf components first: ChannelItem, then MessageItem, building up to the container components.

## Component Plan: Team Messaging Chat Page

### Component Tree
```
ChatPage                    (page — owns all state, orchestrates layout)
├── ChannelSidebar          (channel list)
│   └── ChannelItem         (single channel: name, desc, unread badge)
├── MessageArea             (main message panel)
│   ├── MessageItem         (single message with reactions, hover actions)
│   └── MessageInput        (textarea + send button)
└── ThreadPanel             (collapsible right panel)
    ├── MessageItem         (reused for thread messages)
    └── MessageInput        (reused for reply input)
```

I'll build these components in dependency order, starting with the leaf components like ChannelItem and MessageItem, then composing them into larger panels, and finally orchestrating everything in the ChatPage. I'll also need to add CSS isolation files for styling.

Now I'm setting up the folder structure and thinking through the data flow—ChatPage will manage the main state like channels, selected channel, messages, and thread visibility, then pass down the relevant data and callbacks to each child component. I'll also create a helper method for formatting relative timestamps and organize everything under the project namespace.

Since the service contracts are already registered in DI, I just need to define the types and interface in a Services file for compilation, then start building out the Razor components for the chat interface.

Creating the models and components... Defining the service contracts...

I'm thinking through how to handle thread summaries since the service doesn't expose a dedicated method for them. I can derive thread information from the messages themselves by tracking which messages have replies pointing to them, then maintain that locally in the page component as I load messages. I'll filter the main channel view to show only top-level messages where ReplyToId is null, then compute thread summaries from the complete message list by tracking which messages have replies pointing to them. When a user opens a thread, I'll fetch the full thread details with GetThreadAsync. I'll track thread summaries in a dictionary as messages load, incrementing reply counts for each root message. Then I'll filter the main display to show only top-level messages while keeping replies accessible through the thread panel. Now I'm setting up the component structure with ChannelItem and MessageItem to keep things organized. ThreadPanel needs the root message, reply list, close callback, send reply callback, and react callback. Chat.razor will be the main page that orchestrates everything and manages all the state through IChatService. I'm planning to use CSS isolation for clean component styling, and I'll handle relative time formatting either as a static helper or in the MessageItem code-behind. For the emoji reactions, I'll show reply and react action buttons on message hover, with a small popup displaying common emojis when react is clicked.

Now I'm setting up the directory structure with services, components organized by feature, and pages. I'll start with the leaf components first and build up to the parent containers. For the scrollable message areas, I need to handle loading older messages when scrolling to the top, which will require some JavaScript interop. I'll use an `IntersectionObserver` on a sentinel element at the top of the message list to detect when it comes into view, then trigger loading older messages. I'll create a JS interop module for the MessageArea component and handle scroll position maintenance when new messages load at the top, plus auto-scroll to the bottom for incoming messages. Now let me start writing out all the components, beginning with the service models and working my way up.

Writing service models and component code... Writing the Razor components... Finishing the message actions UI... Setting up the emoji reaction logic with a predefined set of common emojis, toggling the picker visibility, and handling the selection to invoke the callback. I'm also adding a mouse leave handler to close both the action menu and emoji picker, plus starting to implement a relative time formatter for the message timestamps. Writing component logic... Building the message area component... Defining the component parameters... Writing the thread panel component... Writing component markup... Still writing the message loading logic... Finishing the thread filtering, then I'm handling thread closure by clearing the active thread state, and implementing thread replies that add messages to the thread and update the summary with the latest message count and timestamp.

Now I'm working on reaction handling, where I call the service to add a reaction and then update the local message state in both the main messages list and thread messages to reflect the new emoji reaction.

Then I'm building thread summaries by grouping all replies by their parent message ID and storing metadata like reply count and the most recent timestamp for each thread.

I realize I'm passing a `class` parameter to `MessageArea`, but that component doesn't accept it. Instead, I should handle the layout structure in the parent Chat.razor file by wrapping the components in divs with the appropriate CSS classes. I'm restructuring to use a three-column layout where the main column's width adjusts based on whether a thread is active.

Now I'm setting up the CSS with flexbox to manage the layout proportions, defining the sidebar width, making the main column flexible, and setting the thread panel width.

I should add the necessary namespaces to the _Imports.razor file so the Chat components can reference each other properly.

Now I need to think through the `LoadMoreMessages` logic. When loading older messages, they should be inserted at the beginning of the list since they come before the current ones. The skip/take pattern typically returns messages in chronological order, so skipping 50 and taking 50 gives us the next batch of older messages that need to be prepended to what we already have. Now I'm thinking through how to handle the Enter key behavior in the message input. I need to prevent the default newline when Enter is pressed without Shift, but Blazor's conditional prevent-default requires JavaScript interop which adds complexity. For now, I'll simplify and just use the Send button, though the Enter-to-send shortcut would be nice UX. I'll skip the preventDefault complexity and just let the newline appear briefly before the text clears—it's a minor artifact that's acceptable. With `@bind:event="oninput"`, the keydown handler fires first when Enter is pressed, calls Submit to clear the text, and then the input event follows, so the timing should work out cleanly. I'm going to go with the pragmatic approach: use `@bind` with `@onkeydown` together, and when Enter is pressed without Shift, I'll trigger the submit. The newline that briefly appears is acceptable since submitting clears the text anyway and the state updates quickly.

But wait, I'm realizing the input event fires after the keydown handler, which means it could override the cleared text with the newline. I need to prevent that by using `@oninput` instead of `@bind`, and manually manage the text updates in the input handler. I see the issue now — the `@onkeydown:preventDefault` directive gets evaluated at render time, not when the event fires, so setting `preventEnter` in the handler won't actually prevent the default behavior. Rather than dealing with JavaScript interop to fix this properly, I'll just remove the Enter-to-send functionality entirely since the user didn't explicitly request it anyway, and the Send button works fine on its own.

Actually, wait — I'm realizing the real problem with the async event handling in Blazor Server. When Enter is pressed, both the keydown and input events get sent to the server and processed sequentially. The keydown handler calls Submit and clears the text, but then the input event fires afterward and sets the text to just a newline character, since the browser already added it before the default was prevented. So even if I keep the handler, there's a race condition where the newline gets added after the text is cleared.

I'll just remove the Enter-to-send functionality entirely and stick with the button to avoid this mess. Now I need to finalize the implementation and start writing the actual files.

One more thing I should handle: the `MessageArea` component needs to filter messages so it only displays top-level ones where `ReplyToId` is null, since replies should only appear in the thread panel. Though I'm second-guessing whether replies should also show in the main channel with a thread indicator like Slack does, or stay hidden until you open the thread. The requirements mention showing "N replies" on thread starters, but I need to clarify whether the actual reply messages appear in the main feed. I'm deciding between showing all messages in the main list versus filtering to only top-level messages. Showing everything matches the user's description most directly, even though it might create pagination quirks. I'll go with displaying all messages and let thread starters have the "N replies" indicator—the thread panel handles the rest.

Now I'm settling on the file structure: types go in Services, sub-components in Components/Chat, and I'll add an _Imports.razor to keep the using statements clean. I'm going to implement the components directly rather than using a skill since I have a clear plan and the overhead isn't worth it.

Let me start creating all the files now—I'll batch them together and make sure to include the styling for each component so the chat interface looks polished.

Actually, I should check the existing layout first to see if there's a NavMenu I need to handle for the chat page.

data.content:
Build succeeds cleanly. Here's a summary of what was created:

## Component Tree
```
Chat.razor                (page @ /chat — owns all state, calls IChatService)
├── ChannelSidebar        (20% left column)
│   └── ChannelItem       (# name, description snippet, unread badge)
├── MessageArea           (flexible center — 80% or 55% with thread)
│   ├── MessageItem       (author, relative time, content, reactions, hover actions)
│   └── MessageInput      (textarea + Send button)
└── ThreadPanel           (25% right column, shown when thread is open)
    ├── MessageItem       (reused for root + replies)
    └── MessageInput      (reused — Reply button)
```

## Files Created (16 files)
| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `Services/ChatModels.cs` | Records + `IChatService` interface |
| `Components/Chat/ChannelItem.razor(.css)` | Single channel row |
| `Components/Chat/MessageItem.razor(.css)` | Message with reactions, hover actions, emoji picker, thread link |
| `Components/Chat/MessageInput.razor(.css)` | Reusable textarea + submit button |
| `Components/Chat/ChannelSidebar.razor(.css)` | Channel list panel |
| `Components/Chat/MessageArea.razor(.css)` | Message list + header + input + load-more |
| `Components/Chat/ThreadPanel.razor(.css)` | Thread view with root quote, replies, close button |
| `Components/Pages/Chat.razor(.css)` | Page orchestrator — 3-column flex layout |
| `Components/Layout/ChatLayout.razor` | Bare layout (no default nav chrome) |

## Key Behaviors
- **Channel switching** loads messages, marks channel read, clears unread badge
- **Hover actions** reveal Reply + React buttons; React opens a 6-emoji picker
- **Thread panel** opens on Reply/thread-link click, closes with ✕ (main area widens back)
- **Pagination** via "Load older messages" button at top of message list
- **Thread summaries** computed from reply messages; displayed as "N replies" links
- **Relative timestamps** — "just now", "2 min ago", "3h ago", "5d ago", etc.
